coredata.dc:title: B, N, S, Cl doped graphene quantum dots and their effects on gas-sensing properties of Ag-LaFeO3
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yumin
Zhao, Jianhong
Sun, Hongliang
Zhu, Zhongqi
Zhang, Jin
Liu, Qingju
coredata.dc:description: P-type Ag-LaFeO3 (AL) is a promising gas-sensing material owing to its large surface area, rich active oxygen lattice, good thermostability, controllable structure and strong reducibility. While the operating temperature of which is high. In this study, B, N, S and Cl doped graphene quantum dots (GQD) were prepared with a simple liquid-phase exfoliation method and with which decorated AL in order to low the operating temperature of AL. The sensors based on the doped GQD operated at room temperature and showed affinity toward different volatile organic compounds (VOCs). B-GQD revealed p-type semiconductor property and reduced the operating temperature of AL markedly form 90 °C to 55 °C when composite with it because of the synergy effect. Undoped GQD and N, S, Cl doped GQD showed n-type semiconductor property and oppositely increased the operating temperature of AL to different extent when composite with AL. That is, the more electrons in the outer layer of the doped element, the higher the temperature of GQD/AL composite rise. Thus the gas-sensing properties of B-GQD/AL composite was further researched, the results showed that the composite possessed high sensitivity, good selectivity, good stability and low operating temperature to low concentration of formaldehyde.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ag-LaFeO3
p-type GQD
n-type GQD
Formaldehyde sensors
Room temperature

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of composite of doped GQD and AL

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The conventional circuit of a gas sensing tester.
TEM images of (a) GQD, (b) B-GQD, (c) N-GQD, (d) S-GQD, (e) Cl-GQD, (f) AFM image of B-GQD.
TEM image of (a) GQD-AL composites, (b) B-GQD-AL composites, (c) N-GQD-AL composites, (d) S-GQD-AL composites, (e) Cl-GQD-AL composites.
(a) Raman spectra of B-GQD, GQD, N-GQD, S-GQD, Cl-GQD, (b) amplification of the G band of Raman spectra, (c) Raman spectra of the composite of GQD-AL.
(a) Infrared spectrum of GQD, B-GQD, N-GQD, S-GQD, Cl-GQD; infrared fingerprint spectrum (500 1750 cm−1) of (b) GQD, (c) B-GQD, (d) N-GQD, (e) S-GQD, (f) Cl-GQD.
XPS spectra of (a) B-GQD, (b) N-GQD, (c) S-GQD, (d) Cl-GQD.
XPS spectrum of (a) B 1s from B-GQD, (b) N 1s from N-GQD, (c) S 2p from S-GQD, (d) Cl 2p from Cl-GQD.

Response to eight kinds of analytes based on (a) doped GQD, (b) pristine AL, composites of doped GQD and AL; (c) 5 circle of the response to 10 ppm HCHO based on B-GQD-AL, (d) response to 1–30 ppm HCHO based on B-GQD-AL.
A schematic diagram of the proposed reaction mechanism of sensors to HCHO.
Schematics showing the sensing mechanism of (a) B-GQD, (b) B-GQDs-AL.
Resistance and operating temperature of doped GQD with and without compositing of AL.
Comparison of formaldehyde gas sensing performances between the latest reports and this work.

head.ce:title: B, N, S, Cl doped graphene quantum dots and their effects on gas-sensing properties of Ag-LaFeO3

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yunnan Key Laboratory for Micro/nano Materials & Technology, Yunnan University, 650091 Kunming, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Yunnan Key Laboratory for Micro/nano Materials & Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: P-type Ag-LaFeO3 (AL) is a promising gas-sensing material owing to its large surface area, rich active oxygen lattice, good thermostability, controllable structure and strong reducibility. While the operating temperature of which is high. In this study, B, N, S and Cl doped graphene quantum dots (GQD) were prepared with a simple liquid-phase exfoliation method and with which decorated AL in order to low the operating temperature of AL. The sensors based on the doped GQD operated at room temperature and showed affinity toward different volatile organic compounds (VOCs). B-GQD revealed p-type semiconductor property and reduced the operating temperature of AL markedly form 90 °C to 55 °C when composite with it because of the synergy effect. Undoped GQD and N, S, Cl doped GQD showed n-type semiconductor property and oppositely increased the operating temperature of AL to different extent when composite with AL. That is, the more electrons in the outer layer of the doped element, the higher the temperature of GQD/AL composite rise. Thus the gas-sensing properties of B-GQD/AL composite was further researched, the results showed that the composite possessed high sensitivity, good selectivity, good stability and low operating temperature to low concentration of formaldehyde.

ce:section.ce:para: Gas sensors play a vital role in our daily life [1,2]. Typical applications of gas sensors include the detection of toxic analytes and explosive gases for the purpose of public and domestic safety, industrial processes, monitoring of environmental pollution and air quality. A gas sensor working at room temperature is very appealing as it provides low power consumption and no requires of a heater to get high temperature, and hence simplifies the fabrication of sensor devices and reduces the operating cost [3]. Thus, sensors operating at room temperature or at relatively low temperature were significant and required.

In contrast to some single-metal oxide semiconductors, LaFeO3 is a common perovskite-type oxide that exhibits semiconducting behavior [4] and is a promising material with an abundance of functionalities, especially in the field of gas sensing. In our previous work [5], LaFeO3 is doped with Ag, some Ag is present that can act as a catalyst in the matrix. Indeed, some of the Ag fills areas between the grains of the matrix and works to decrease the contact potential barrier and enhance the interfacial effects, leading to a lower resistance, and thus, a lower operating temperature (90 °C). AL also shows good selectivity, high sensitivity and good stability to 1 ppm formaldehyde.

Graphene quantum dots (GQD) are the 0D nanomaterial with single or few-layer graphene, which has emerged as a promising material by virtue of the unique response of energy band gap, photoluminescence and electron transport ability to the change of geometric size. Owing to these unique properties, the GQD have attracted an increasing interest in the application of energy conversion (e.g. solar energy [6], chemical energy [7]), photodetector [8], as well as sensor (e.g. electronic sensor [9], photoluminescence sensor [10], electrochemiluminescence sensors [11], and electrochemical sensor [12]). Even though GQD are being widely used in the field of sensors, however, it is still the early stage of development for applications in VOCs gas sensor, which has a huge potential in indoor environmental monitoring [13,14]. In comparison with the VOCs gas sensor made of metal oxides, such as SnO2, WO3 and ZnO, which need high operating temperatures in the range from 200 °C to 400 °C in order to activate the adsorption and desorption of VOCs [15,16], the VOCs gas sensors by GQD can operate at room temperature [17–19]. However, the applications of GODs-based sensors may be still hindered because of the drawbacks related to low sensitivity, slow response and poor selectivity. Selective detection of a particular gas in a gas mixture by GQD-based gas sensor has seldom been investigated, and the selectivity of these sensors is required to be improved. The sensing mechanism of most GQD-based sensors is physical or chemical adsorption of target gases. A variety of gas molecules can be adsorbed on GQD sensing layers to give similar conductivity changes. For example, the adsorption of an oxidizing gas, either NO2 or SO2 increases the conductance of graphene, meanwhile exposure to a reducing gas such as NH3 or H2S causes the decrease in conductance. As a result, different gases may generate the same sensing signals. Element-doping of GQD is a facile approach to solve this problem. A selective detection could be achieved by element-doping of GQD with capture agents that can specifically bind with the target gas.

In this study, we aim to achieve formaldehyde gas sensor with good selectivity, high sensitivity, good stability as well as lower operating temperature. Therefore, doped GQD, the new semiconductor with high carrier mobility were chosen to cooperate with AL. The semiconductor behavior of GQD doped with B, N, S and Cl were explored and the impact of those semiconductor behaviors on the operating temperature of AL was studied. It is found that B-GQD with p-type semiconductor behavior could have synergy effect with p-type AL and thus reduced the operating temperature to 55 °C which is much lower than the lately reports and is very close to room temperature.

All the chemical reagents used in the experiments were purchased without further purification. High-purity reagents (e.g. NMP) (99.9%) were purchased from Tianjin FengChuan chemical reagent Technology Co. Ltd.

GQD were prepared using a liquid-phase exfoliation method. Degreasing cotton was directly put into a 1000 °C oven and fully carbonized into carbon fibers (CF) after heated in air atmosphere for 30 min. The CF was ground adequately. 0.5 g CF was added into 80 mL NMP as solution A. The solution A was treated by ultrasonic dispersion machine for 10 h. The resultant dispersion was centrifuged for 45 min at 4000 rpm. After centrifugation, the top clear solution was the GQD solution.
To prepare B, N, S, Cl doped GQD, CF need to be preprocessed. Other than the CF-preprocessing, the rest of the procedures are the same. The CF-preprocessing respectively illustrated as follows:

In this case, 5 g of CF was treated with boric acid (H3BO3 sat., 100 mL), B and oxygen functional groups were introduced on layer-structured CF. Here, H3BO3 plays two roles: One is to provide a B doping source; the other is to oxidize and cut the CF into small pieces. After that, turbid liquid was washed by distilled water until the pH reached neutral. Then, the boracic CF (0.5 g) was added into 80 mL NMP to attain B-GQD.

5 g of CF was treated with nitric acid (HNO3, 17 mol L−1, 100 mL), N and oxygen functional groups were introduced on layer-structured CF. Here, HNO3 plays two roles: One is to provide a N doping source; the other is to oxidize and cut the CF into small pieces. After that, turbid liquid was washed by distilled water until the pH reached neutral. Then, the nitrogenous CF (0.5 g) was added into 80 mL NMP to attain N-GQD.

5 g of CF was chlorinated with hydrochloric acid (HCl, 6 mol L−1, 100 mL), halogen and oxygen functional groups were introduced on layered structure CF. Here, HCl plays two roles: One is to provide a Cl doping source; the other is to oxidize and cut the CF into small pieces. After that, turbid liquid was washed by distilled water until the pH reached neutral. Then, the chlorinated CF (0.5 g) was added into 80 mL NMP to attain Cl-GQD.

The preprocessing of sulf-CF is different from the above three. To start with, commercial graphite (5 g) and sulfur (5 g) were placed into an agate jar containing agate ball. Then, the jar was sealed and charged with argon after several charging-discharging cycles. Thereafter, the jar was fixed in a planetary ball-mill machine (TCI, USA) and agitated at 500 rpm for 48 h. Finally, the resultant powders were obtained and denoted as sulf-CF. 0.5 g sulf-CF was added into 80 mL NMP to attain S-GQD.

AL was prepared using a sol–gel method combining with microwave chemical synthesis. All the chemicals used are analytic grade reagents without further purifications from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagents Development Center. 0.99 mmol La(NO3)3·6H2O, 1.00 mmol Fe(NO3)3·9H2O and 1.00 mmol citrate were dissolved in 100 mL distilled water as solution A. 0.01 mmol AgNO3 were dissolved in 10 mL distilled water and added to the solution A, and then polyethylene glycol (PEG) was added. The final mixed solution was kept stirring at 80 °C for 8 h, and then was put in the microwave chemical device to synthesize for 2 h. The sol was dried at 150 °C and ground, and then heat treated at 800 °C for 2 h to get AL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of composite of doped GQD and AL
ce:section.ce:para: The weight ratio of doped GQD and AL was 1:50. 0.01 g doped GQD powder and 0.5 g AL were mixed and added in 10 mL distilled water, treated by ultrasonic concussion for 30 min and stirred for 12 h and then dried.

The prepared materials were further mixed with distilled water respectively and ground to form a paste, which was subsequently printed onto an alumina tube. There are two Au electrodes placed at the both end sides of the tube. The length of the alumina tube is 4 mm and the diameter is 1.2 mm. In order to improve their stability and repeatability, the gas sensors were aged at 150 °C for 170 h in air. The gas-sensing properties were test by WS-30A gas senor tester. The export voltage of the sensor was measured by using a conventional circuit (Scheme 1 ) in which the element was connected with an external resistor in series at a circuit voltage of 5 V. According to Scheme 1, the electrical resistance of sensor can be obtained as follows:
(1)
R =
5 −
V
0
V
0
⋅
R L
,
where R is the resistance of the sensor, RL is the constant load resistance unchanged with the surrounding gas partial pressure, and V0 is the sensor export voltage. For p-type semiconductor, the gas response (β) was defined as the ratio of the electrical resistance in gas (Rg) to that in air (Ra):
(2)
R g
=
5 −
(
V
0
)
gas
(
V
0
)
gas
⋅
R L
,
(3)
R a
=
5 −
(
V
0
)
air
(
V
0
)
air
⋅
R L
,
(4)
β =
R
g
R a
,
where (V0)air is the export voltage in air, and (V0)gas is in gases (formaldehyde, toluene, acetone and methanol). The gas-sensing properties were examined in a chamber though which air or a sample gases (formaldehyde, toluene, acetone and methanol diluted with air) were allowed to flow at a rate of 160 cm3 min−1. The electrical response of the Ag-LaFeO3 sensor was measured with an automatic test system, controlled by a personal computer.

The morphology of synthesized GQD was examined by transmission electron microscopy (Tecnai G2 TF30) and atomic force microscope (SPA-400). The Raman spectrum was recorded at ambient temperature on a Renishaw inVia Raman microscope with an argon-ion laser at an excitation wavelength of 514.5 nm. Photoluminescence were characterized by fluorescence spectrometers (Edinburgh FS5). Functional groups were verified by XPS (PHI VersaProbe II) using 50W AlKα radiation.

The micro morphologies of the as prepared GQD are shown via transmission electron microscope (TEM) images and atomic force microscope (AFM) in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1a shows the undoped GQD. As shown in the figure, three specific lattice spacing of graphene are observed, which are 0.246 nm, 0.213 nm and 0.335 nm, respectively [20]. The size of the GQD is about 3–5 nm. After GQD doped with B, N, S, Cl, the interlamellar spacing enlarged to 0.346 nm (B-GQD, Fig. 1b), 0.340 nm (N-GQD, Fig. 1c), 0.344 nm (S-GQD, Fig. 1d) and 0.353 nm (Cl-GQD, Fig. 1e), respectively. The diameter of the doped GQD is less than 10 nm. Fig. 1f shows the AFM image of B-GQD, which is in concert with Fig. 1b revealing the diameter of B-GQD is 2–4 nm. The TEM images of the composites of AL and GQD are shown in Fig. 2 . It can be seen from Fig. 2a–e that the GQD are invisible with AL because the big differences in size.

Fig. 3 a is the Raman spectra of the GQD before and after doped with B, N, S, Cl. The 2D band consists of a single Lorentzian [21,22]. In addition, a D and D’ band were observed, which attributed to the defective graphene edges formed during the element doping [22]. These defect peaks are not present in bulk single-layer graphene samples [23]. Fig. 3b is amplification of the G band of Raman spectra. The large peak redshift (Δn = 14 cm−1, N-GQD; Δν = 25 cm−1, S-GQD; Δν = 4 cm−1, Cl-GQD) indicates that N, S, Cl can stiffen the Raman scattering frequencies of G and 2D peaks of GQD, resulting in the n-type doping [24,25]. While, a large peak blue shift appears in the spectrum of B-GQD (Δν = 26 cm−1) which indicates that B can soften the Raman scattering frequencies, resulting in the p-type doping [24,25]. Because of the doping process, oxygen containing groups, including carbonyl, hydroxyl groups, and specific functional groups (-B, -N, -S, -Cl) were introduced at the edges and onto the basal plane, The presence of these groups makes the GQD soluble in water and other solvents. Then the Raman spectrum of the composite of GQDs-AL was achieved which containing both the bands of AL and GQDs. The peaks at 285 and 461 cm−1 corresponds to Ag and B3g distribution pattern. The peak at 629 cm−1 is due to two photons and the scattering related to the impurity. These peaks are consistent with the reported LaFeO3 [26]. And the G band, D band and 2D band were attributed to GQDs as mentioned above.

The infrared spectrum of GQD, B-GQD, N-GQD, S-GQD, Cl-GQD are shown in Fig. 4 . It can be seen from the figure, the strong absorption band located near 1620 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration absorption of sp2 (CC) structure of graphene quantum dot, the band located at 3440 cm−1 corresponding to stretching vibration of OH [27]. In addition, there are a few weak absorption peaks which are asymmetric stretching vibrations of the CC bond (1120 cm−1) and the stretching vibration of the CH bond (615 cm−1, 1402 cm−1, 2915 cm−1) respectively [28]. To further distinguish the molecular structure of undoped and doped GQD, we enlarged fingerprint zone (500–1750 cm−1) of the spectrum as shown in Fig. 4b–f. It can be seen from Fig. 4b that there are several weak peaks besides the obvious peaks mentioned above. For example, the absorption peak of 1302 cm−1 belongs to the OH bond in the in-plane deformation of vibration. The absorption peak around 1507 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of the CC bond and the absorption peak at 1716 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibration of the CO bond [29]. This indicates that hydroxyl, carboxyl and other functional groups will be introduced in the preparation of graphene quantum dots. Fig. 4c is the infrared fingerprint spectrum of B-GQD, as can be seen from the figure, two additional vibrational absorption peaks correspond to the stretching vibrations of the CB bond (1138, cm−1) and the BO bond (1340 cm−1) appeared [30], indicating the effective doping of B element. Fig. 4d is the infrared spectrum of N-GQD, two additional vibrational absorption peaks at 860 cm−1 (NH) and 1048 cm−1 (CN) are shown. Three additional absorption peaks exist in the infrared spectra of S-GQD (Fig. 4e), they are vibrational absorption peaks of CS (720, cm−1), CO-S (782, cm−1) and SO (1315, cm−1) bonds, respectively. Fig. 4f is the infrared spectrum of Cl-GQD, from which two weak peaks of vibration at 796 cm−1 and 1050 cm−1 can be seen, they are all corresponding to the stretching vibration absorb of CCl [29,31].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out to further confirm the functional groups in the doped GQD. As seen in Fig. 5 , the XPS spectra show a dominant graphitic C1s peak around 284.5 eV and O1s peak around 531 eV for the doped GQD. N1s peak (400 eV) is from residual NMP [32]. In the case of N-GQD, the intensity of N1s is much stronger than the other three, which is also attributed to the N source (nitride acid) [33]. Other than C1s and O1s, XPS spectra also confirmed the presence of B1s, S2p and Cl2p, respectively.

The high-resolution XPS spectra of B1s, N1s, S2p and Cl2p are shown in Fig. 6 , the bonding type of the doping elements can be observed. Based on the Shirley algorithm, we separately simulate the peaks; obviously, the B1s high resolution spectrogram is overlapped by two peaks of energy spectrum. The spectral peak located at 190.3 eV corresponds to BC2O groups, proving that boron is doped with GQD in epoxy form. The other one located at 191.9 eV is due to the presence of small amounts of boronic acid groups (BCO2) [30,34,35]. N1s high resolution spectrogram contains three peaks of energy spectrum, which shows that the nitrogen atoms are in the presence of pyridine nitrogen (399.8 eV), pyrrole nitrogen (400.4 eV) and graphite nitrogen (401.6 eV) in GQD [34–38]. Among them, the content of pyridine nitrogen is the highest, which explained that most of the nitrogen substituted carbon atoms still retain the original hexagonal lattice of GQD, no larger lattice distortion was found. This is consistent with the characterization of Raman spectra. S2p high resolution spectrogram consists of four peaks, which is mainly due to spin splitting of S2p energy levels. Moreover, S exists mainly in two forms in GQD: yhiophene S (-C-S-C-) and oxidized S (-C-SO2-C-) [36,39,40]. Four independent peaks are obtained by peak fitting of Cl2p high resolution spectrogram, it shows that Cl has four different bonding forms in GQD, including −OCCl (198.2, eV), −OC6Cl4O (199.3, eV), −C6H4Cl (200.5, eV) and −CH2Cl (201.9, eV) [31,41].

The most accepted mechanism for gas sensing in p-type semiconductor materials, such as B-GQD or AL, involves the adsorption of oxygen species on its surface [42]. Electrons are injected into the conduction band and then coupled with holes in the valence band, producing a decrease in the conductivity. So, for them, the response is calculated via Rg/Ra. On the contrary, n-type semiconductor, such as undoped GQD, N-GQD, S-GQD, Cl-GQD, would produce an increase in the conductivity. So, for them, the response is calculated via Ra/Rg.

To confirm the gas selectivity of the as-fabricated sensors based on doped GQD. The response of doped GQD to acetone, methanol, benzene, ammonia, gasoline, ethanol, toluene and formaldehyde at room temperature were tested and showed in Fig. 7 a. It can be seen from Fig. 7a, B-GQD tend to selectively recognize methanol and formaldehyde because of the OH and COOH on the edge of the GQD [43]. The undoped GQD didn’t show any preference to the analytes, the responses to the 8 analytes are even. N-GQD are sensitive to methanol because of the amount of COOH [44]. S-GQD are sensitive to ammonia, which in accordance of the earlier reports [45]. And Cl-GQD prefer to formaldehyde. After the doped GQD composite with AL, the ideal formaldehyde sensing material, the selectivity and resistance of AL are affected. The responses of the composites to acetone, methanol, benzene, ammonia, gasoline, ethanol, toluene and formaldehyde are shown in Fig. 7b, and the resistance and the operating temperature are shown in Table 1 .

Owing to the size of the GQD, when composite with AL, nano-sized GQD coated on the surface of AL, so, the selectivity of AL is more or less affected by the doped GQD. It can be seen form Fig. 2, GQD are invisible with AL since the large size difference. Some of the GQD bridge between AL grain boundaries which play the role of electron conductors and form the shortened transport paths. So, they improve the electrical properties and charge transfer of AL. When AL composited with B-GQD, the formaldehyde gas sensing properties (sensitivity and operating temperature) are improved because of the synergistic effect. Especially the operating temperature which decreased to 55 °C. But when AL composited with other n-type GQD, the resistance and operating temperature increased to a large extent. This is because AL and GQD couldn’t form p-n junction for the unmatched barrier, what’s more, the hole from AL and the electron from GQD would recombine to further increase the system resistance. Consequently, p-type B-GQD is the promising materials to composite with p-type metal oxide semiconductor gas sensing materials. As a result, the stability, response and recovery of B-GQD-AL based sensor was further researched. Fig. 7c shows the responses of the sensor based on B-GQD-AL to 10 ppm formaldehyde (HCHO) for 5 cycles. It is found that the response can recover its initial state after immersing into air from HCHO, indicating the high operating stability of the B-GQD-AL based gas sensor. Fig. 7d shows the response of B-GQD-AL sensor, varying with different concentrations of HCHO. The response value of the sensor increases when HCHO concentration is increased. The response time is 20–35s with the HCHO concentration ranging from 1 to 30 ppm while the recovery time is 30–130 s along the increasing of HCHO concentration. After the sensor has recovered about 90%, it became slower. The reason is when the sensor recovered 90%, the concentration of the residual HCHO on the surface of the materials is low, the interaction between this part (the last 10%) of HCHO and sensor is chemisorption [46]; it is very difficult for those HCHO to desorb from the surface [47,48]. So, the higher the HCHO concentration is, the slower the recovery is.

Compared with gasoline, methanol and toluene, formaldehyde is much more reductive and thus causes a larger B-GQDs-AL resistance change by reacting with a larger amount of adsorbed oxygen. Gasoline, methanol, ethanol and toluene are less reductive than formaldehyde, and therefore their reactions with adsorbed oxygen might be harder, leading to a lower resistance change at the same temperature [49]. Ammonia and acetone are also reductive, but it should be noted that they are not easily oxidized by a metal oxide catalyst as shown in some studies [50,51], which might account for the low response of B-GQDs-AL to ammonia and acetone. Therefore, the as prepared B-GQDs-AL exhibits good selectivity to formaldehyde. Compared with the latest reports in Table 2 , formaldehyde gas sensor based on B-GQD-AL possesses high response and selectivity, low operating temperature and good stability.

As described before, AL is typical p-type semiconductors in air, its gas-sensing mechanism is based on the changes of the resistance before and after being exposed to the test gas. As seen in Fig. 8 , the oxygen adsorbed on the surface directly influences the resistance of the AL-based sensors.

In the calcined process at high temperature, LaFeO3 lost the La atoms at the crank pints of cells and left vacancies. Their main charge-carrier holes (
h •
) are produced by the ionization of the La3+ cation vacancies [61]:
(1)
V La x
→
V
L a
′ ′ ′
+ 3
h •

When the sensors based on AL are exposed to the air by heating, the oxygen chemically adsorbed on the surface undergoes the following reactions [62]:
(2)
O
2 ( a d )
↔
O
2 ( a d )
−
↔ 2
O
( a d )
−
↔ 2
O
( a d )
2 −

When operating temperature increases, the equilibrium shifts to the right. The oxygen captures electrons from the material, leading to the increase in concentration of holes. Hence, the resistance of the sensors decreased due to the increasing concentration of available carrier. On the other hand, when the sensors were exposed to the reducing gases such as acetone, toluene, methanol, ethanol or formaldehyde in our study, the interaction between the reducing gas and the oxygen adsorbed on the sensors surface can be explained as [62]:
(3)
R +
O
( ad )
n −
↔ R O + n
e
′

Taking HCHO as example, it was found that formaldehyde gas can be decomposed to CO2, H2O, and produce electrons at the same time. The possible reactions took place on the surface of AL as follows [63]:
(4)
H C H
O
( a d )
+ 2
O
( a d )
2 −
→ C
O
2
+
H
2
O
( g )
+ 4
e ′

The electrons trapped by the adsorbed oxygen are released. So, a number of electrons are annihilated with holes [64]:
(5)
h •
+
e ′
= n ull

With the gas concentration increasing, this action enhances until saturation. Consequently, both the concentration of the holes in AL and conductivity of the sensors decreased. Therefore the changes in resistance determine the gas response of the sensors.

Fig. 9 shows the sensing schematic of the B-GQD and B-GQDs-AL. The gas sensing mechanism of B-GQD attributed to the adsorption/desorption of sensing species, which act as donors or acceptors on the B-GQD surface and edge, leading to the change in conductivity of B-GQD [65] as showed in Fig. 9a. Taking HCHO as example, the sensing mechanism of B-GQD as adsorption of HCHO with the surface ionic conductivity changes. During oxidation process, −COOH functional groups are attached at the edge of the B-GQD, leading to protonation of carboxyl groups [66,67]. When B-GQD is exposed to HCHO gas, the functional −COOH tends to ionize to COO− and H+, then COO− and H+ ion reacts with HCHO gas form electrons [68], thus increased the resistance of the p-type B-GQD and then increased the gas response (Rg/Ra).

When B-GQD composited with AL, B-GQDs will bridge between AL grain boundaries like Fig. 9b shows. So, in the air, the conductivity of B-GQDs-AL is high because of the high concentration of holes resulting in a low Ra and low operating temperature. B-GQDs also plays the role of energy band bridge between HCHO and AL because the Ec of B-GQDs is 4.2 eV [69] which is between HCHO (3.9 eV [70]) and AL (4.7 eV [71]). When HCHO contact B-GQDs-AL, it reacts with both AL and B-GQDs to form electrons; those electrons could easily transport in the composite and recombined with the holes to get a high Rg making a high response (Rg/Ra). The same electronic properties and gas sensing mechanism lead to the synergy effect to enhance the gas response and reduce the operating temperature.

In conclusion, we demonstrated a simple solution fabrication route to obtain GQD and doped GQD. The semiconductor properties of the doped GQD are opposite with two kinds of doping, one is doping with the element before carbon-group element (B) and the other one is doping with the element after carbon-group element (N, S, Cl) in the periodic table of elements. B-doping exhibit p-type semiconductor property, while N-, S-, Cl- doping exhibit n-type semiconductor property. Doped GQD show affinity towards different VOC gases because of the different functional groups introduced via different element doping. Sensors based on GQD or doped GQD operate at room temperature on account of the high carrier mobility. When composite with AL, a p-type semiconductor, the p-type B-GQD showed synergy effect with AL and improved both the gas response and operating temperature of AL. The sensor based on B-GQD-AL composite possessed good selectivity, high sensitivity and good stability to formaldehyde. The operating temperature is of 55 °C which is very low compared with the sensors based on other semiconductors in the previous reports. Therefore, GQD attached with functional groups can be used solely or as modified materials to realize VOCs gas sensors with high gas responses and low operating temperature.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51562038 and 51402257), the Natural Science Foundation of Yunnan Province, China (No. 2013FZ003), Yunnan Basic Applied Research Project (no. 2017FB086).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Y.M. Zhang received her BS in Department of Materials Science and Engineering from Yunnan University in 2011. She received her MS in 2014 and PhD in 2017 in Yunnan University. She devotes to gas sensors for formaldehyde.
J.H Zhao received his BS in Department of Materials Science and Engineering from Yunnan University in 2011. He received his MS in Yunnan University in 2015. He devotes to perovskite solar cell.
H.L. Sun received his BS in 2000 and MS in 2003 in Kunming University of Science and Technology. He is working on his PhD in Xi’an Jiaotong University.

Z.Q. Zhu obtained his MS degree in environmental engineering from Kunming University of Science and Technology in 2003. His current research deals with nanostructure functional materials and the applications.
J. Zhang received her MS in applied chemistry from Yunnan University in 2002. Her current fields of interest are gas sensor based on semiconductor oxides and thin film.

Q.J. Liu is a professor in School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yunnan University. She received her MS in Optoelectronic Science and Engineering from Huazhong University of Science and Technology in 1990 and PhD in Materials Science from Kunming University of Science and Technology in 2003. Her work is devoted to gas sensors, nanostructured functional materials and their applications in entironment engineering.
